Bluebell.toxicity: Highly Toxic if ingested by humans or pets
Bluebell.fact: Protected by law in the UK; picking wild bluebells can carry heavy fines.
Bluebell.care: Thrives best in partial shade with moist, rich, organic woodland soil environments.

Buttercup.toxicity: Toxic to livestock and humans when fresh
Buttercup.meaning: Good cheer, youthfulness, and radiant charm
Buttercup.fact: Their glossy appearance is caused by a unique mirror-like cell structure reflecting yellow light.
Buttercup.care: Highly resilient. Requires open sunlight access and moist to wet garden soils.

Coltsfoot.toxicity: Contains alkaloids; potentially toxic to the liver long-term
Coltsfoot.fact: The flowers emerge and die back entirely before its green leaves even begin to sprout.
Coltsfoot.care: Extremely rugged, wild plant. Prefers open direct sun and damp clay-rich ground conditions.

Cowslip.uses: Culinary flavoring, wine making, traditional medicine
Cowslip.fact: In old folklore, cowslips are traditionally believed to hold the keys that unlock hidden treasures.
Cowslip.care: Requires damp, neutral to alkaline soil setups. Enjoys open sun positions or partial light shade.

Crocus.toxicity: Autumn Crocus is highly toxic; Spring variety is safe
Crocus.fact: Saffron, the world's costliest culinary spice, is harvested by hand from crocus filaments.
Crocus.care: Requires porous, sandy soil layers. Plant bulbs where they get plenty of ambient daytime light.

Daffodil.toxicity: Toxic to companion animals and humans
Daffodil.fact: Squirrels and deer avoid eating daffodil bulbs because they contain crystals that act as a natural deterrent.
Daffodil.care: Prefers sunny or lightly shaded garden patches; requires damp soil conditions during early growth.

Daisy.fact: Daisies represent a composite structure; the center yellow disc is actually a collection of many miniature flowers.
Daisy.care: Extremely straightforward care matrix. Ensure access to open daily sunlight and light regular waterings.

Fritillary.toxicity: Highly Toxic due to poisonous alkaloids
Fritillary.fact: The unique checkerboard pattern on its hanging bell-shaped flowers is completely natural.
Fritillary.care: Requires rich, wet soils that never dry out completely. Prefers organic shaded locations.

Iris.toxicity: Toxic to dogs, cats, and humans
Iris.meaning: Wisdom, valor, faith, and cherished trust
Iris.fact: Named after the ancient Greek goddess Iris, who traveled along rainbows as a cosmic messenger.
Iris.care: Requires high-exposure sun zones and dry, well-aerated soil structures to protect the root rhizomes.

Lily.sunlight: Shaded base roots with sunlit upper flowers
Lily.meaning: Purity of spirit, majesty, and renewal
Lily.fact: True lilies grow from solid scaly bulbs, distinguishing them from daylilies or water lilies.
Lily.care: Keep root systems sheltered and cool while ensuring upper flower heads face direct ambient sunlight paths.

Pansy.fact: Pansies are remarkably cold-hardy and can survive freezing winter weather to bloom again in spring.
Pansy.care: Loves mild climates. Keep soil damp, nutrient-dense, and clean of decaying vegetation elements.

Sunflower.uses: Agricultural oil production, edible seed harvesting
Sunflower.fact: Immature sunflower buds perform daily heliotropic tracking, adjusting face vectors east-to-west to maximize light absorption.
Sunflower.care: Requires high nutrient soil spaces and continuous deep watering cycles while growing large.
